chunk_summary: Postpyloric feeding is recommended for children with high aspiration risk or failed gastric feeding, emphasizing continuous jejunal feeding and monitoring clinical tolerance over gastric residual volume checks.
learning_objectives: Understand indications for postpyloric feeding in pediatric patients.
Learn the physiological differences and safety considerations between gastric and jejunal feeding.
Identify clinical signs of feeding intolerance and appropriate monitoring strategies.
Recognize triggers for escalation from gastric to postpyloric feeding access.
key_facts: Postpyloric feeding is indicated for aspiration risk, recurrent emesis, or poor gastric emptying.
Jejunal feeds are typically delivered continuously to avoid dumping syndrome.
Dumping syndrome symptoms include tachycardia, diaphoresis, diarrhea, and abdominal pain.
Iso-osmolar feeds (<300 mOsm/kg) are better tolerated than hyperosmolar mixtures.
Gastric residual volume (GRV) checks are not useful for jejunal feeding; clinical endpoints like stool output and respiratory status should be monitored.
Escalation triggers include recurrent aspiration pneumonia and failure to meet nutritional needs due to frequent fasting.
reference_quote: Postpyloric feeding is not a 'stronger' version of gastric feeding; it changes physiology and safety rules.
document_key_topics: Monitoring feeding tolerance in pediatric patients
Escalation criteria for enteral feeding access
